summary: Gruber analyzes Steven Sinofsky's repeated emphasis on Windows 8 being a 'no compromise' design that combines tablet and desktop experiences in one OS. He contrasts this with Apple's deliberate embrace of compromise in iOS, arguing that iOS's limitations are actually what make the iPad better than a Mac for many use cases. Gruber acknowledges he personally uses a MacBook Air for work because the iPad's compromises slow him down, but praises the iPad for enabling things Macs were never good at. He remains skeptical that Microsoft can pull off its ambitious 'one OS for all machines' approach, calling it a 'boil-the-ocean plan' with too big an 'if.'
key_points: Microsoft's Windows 8 strategy emphasizes 'no compromise' by combining Metro touch interface with full desktop capabilities in one OS
Apple took the opposite approach: iOS's deliberate compromises enforce simplicity, obvious design, and excellent battery life
The iPad's value isn't replacing a Mac — it's opening doors to use cases Macs were never good at
Gruber sees Windows 8's unified approach as overly ambitious and fundamentally risky, despite its potential upside
money_quotes: The compromises in iOS are, for many people in many contexts, what makes the iPad better than a Mac.
The compromises enforce simplicity and obviousness in design, and at a technical level they lead to iOS's excellent battery life.
The marvel of the iPad is not that it can replace a Mac. It's that it opened the door to all sorts of things that a Mac was never all that good for.
What it comes down to is that I think their goal is too ambitious. It's a boil-the-ocean plan.
But if they pull it off, they might really have something. I just think it's too big an 'if'.
key_insight: Deliberate compromise in product design can be a strength rather than a weakness, because constraints enforce simplicity and open new possibilities that an everything-for-everyone approach cannot.
